column3: Add thumbs up / thumbs down buttons on the result panel. Store each piece of feedback in a SQLite table alongside the log chunk and LLM response. This data lets you iterate on your system prompt over time — and in an interview, it shows you think about ML feedback loops, not just one-shot predictions.
column4:
# backend/feedback.py
import sqlite3, datetime

DB = 'feedback.db'

def init_db():
    con = sqlite3.connect(DB)
    con.execute('''
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS feedback (
            id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            log_chunk TEXT,
            llm_response TEXT,
            rating INTEGER,
            created_at TEXT
        )''')
    con.commit(); con.close()

def save_feedback(log_chunk, llm_response, rating):
    con = sqlite3.connect(DB)
    con.execute(
        'INSERT INTO feedback VALUES (NULL,?,?,?,?)',
        (log_chunk, llm_response, rating,
         datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    )
    con.commit(); con.close()
column5: SQLite requires zero infrastructure — no Docker service, no credentials. Perfect for a portfolio project. Swap to Postgres only when you actually need concurrent writes.

column3: Write a Dockerfile for the FastAPI backend and a separate one for the React frontend (built with Nginx). A docker-compose.yml wires them together so anyone can spin up the whole project with one command. Deploy to EC2 (free tier) or Railway for a live URL you can demo in interviews.
column4:
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.9'
services:
  backend:
    build: ./backend
    ports: ['8000:8000']
    env_file: .env
    volumes: ['./feedback.db:/app/feedback.db']
  frontend:
    build: ./frontend
    ports: ['3000:80']
    depends_on: [backend]

# backend/Dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ['uvicorn', 'main:app', '--host', '0.0.0.0', '--port', '8000']

# frontend/Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json .
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
column5: A live URL changes the interview dynamic. Instead of describing the project, you open your laptop and show it. That's a strong signal for a product-first company like Consuma.

column3: Write a README that covers: what the project does, a simple architecture diagram, how to run it locally, and three 'engineering decisions' paragraphs — why you chunked tokens, why you added the pre-filter, and why you chose streaming. These three paragraphs are what will actually get discussed in your technical interview.
column4:
# README.md structure

## LLM Debugging Tool
> Paste a log file, get a root cause analysis in seconds.

## Architecture
  React (Vite) --> FastAPI --> OpenAI API
                          --> Pattern pre-filter (local, free)
                          --> SQLite (feedback store)

## Run locally
  cp .env.example .env   # add OPENAI_API_KEY
  docker compose up

## Key engineering decisions

### 1. Token chunking
Long logs are filtered to ERROR/WARN lines, then truncated to 3000
tokens using tiktoken. This keeps costs predictable and avoids
context-window errors.

### 2. Pattern pre-filter
Known error signatures (OOMKilled, ECONNREFUSED) return a local
result without hitting the LLM. Saves ~$0.001/request and responds
in <5ms vs ~2s.

### 3. Streaming
The backend streams tokens via SSE so the UI updates in real-time
rather than waiting for the full response.
column5: The 'engineering decisions' section is the most important part of your README. It's what interviewers read to assess seniority — anyone can call an API, few can explain the tradeoffs they made.
